---
title: "ANCOVA — Analysis of Covariance"
subtitle: "Covariates"
description: "Analysis of covariance mixing continuous and categorical predictors."
categories: [ANOVA, Regression]
order: 15
week: 8
author: "Bill Perry"

execute: 
  collapse: true
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    paged.print: false
    collapse: true
format:
  html:
    downloads: [docx, pptx]  # This creates download links for all three
  # RevealJS - UNCHANGED (keeps your two-column layout and large images)
  revealjs:
    output-ext: "slides.html"
  docx: default
  pptx: default
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
# install.packages("interactions")
library(interactions)
library(car)
library(emmeans)
library(patchwork)
library(broom)
library(gridExtra)
library(tidyverse)

# for type 3 anova
options(contrasts = c("contr.sum", "contr.poly")) # for true type 3 anovas

```

# Lecture 14: Review

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Review**

-   General Linear Models (GLM)
-   Gaussian GLMs (normal distribution)
-   Poisson GLMs (count data)
-   Logistic GLMs (binary outcomes)
-   Model assumptions and diagnostics
-   Connection to ANOVA and linear models
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
# Placeholder for review image
```
:::
:::::

# Lecture 15: ANCOVA Overview

**Overview**

-   Analysis of covariance (ANCOVA):
    -   Introduction to ANCOVA

    -   The ANCOVA workflow in R

    -   When to use ANCOVA

    -   Building the ANCOVA model in R

    -   Assumptions of ANCOVA

        -   Homogeneity of slopes (The critical test!)

    -   Interpreting results & "Adjusted Means"

    -   Handling violations (heterogeneous slopes)

# Introduction to ANCOVA

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
**What is ANCOVA?**

-   ANCOVA = Analysis of COVAriance
    -   It's a combination of regression and ANOVA
    -   It's used when you have:
        -   A continuous response variable (Y)

        -   A categorical predictor (Factor A)

        -   A continuous predictor (Covariate X)
    -   **Common use:**
        -   Compare means of factor levels (groups), after statistically
            adjusting for the effect of the continuous covariate.
    -   **Another use:**
        -   Determine whether two or more regression lines differ in
            slopes and/or intercepts.
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false

# Create a simple dataset to illustrate ANCOVA
set.seed(123)
n <- 60
group <- rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = n/3)
x <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 1.5)
y <- 2 + 0.8 * x + ifelse(group == "A", 2, ifelse(group == "B", 1, 0)) + rnorm(n, sd = 1)
data <- data.frame(group = group, x = x, y = y)

# Plot with regression lines by group
ggplot(data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "ANCOVA: Regression Lines by Group",
       x = "Covariate (X)",
       y = "Response Variable (Y)",
       color = "Group") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::
:::::

# When to Use ANCOVA

-   **Common Applications of ANCOVA**
    -   **Increasing statistical power**
        -   The covariate (e.g., body size, initial temperature)
            explains some of the "noise" (residual error) in the
            response variable.

        -   By "removing" this variation, we reduce the residual error,
            making it easier to detect the true effect of our
            categorical factor.

        -   This results in a more powerful test of treatment effects.
    -   **Adjusting for confounding variables**
        -   Imagine your treatment groups happen to differ in their
            average covariate value (e.g., Group A was tested in a
            warmer room than Group B).

        -   ANCOVA allows you to separate the treatment effect from the
            covariate effect.
    -   **Testing equality of regression lines**
        -   Do treatments have the same relationship (slope) with a
            continuous variable?

# ANCOVA Example: Cricket Chirping

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Cricket Chirping Example**

-   Want to compare chirping rate of two cricket species:
    -   *Oecanthus exclamationis*
    -   *Oecanthus niveus*
-   **But...**
    -   Measured rates at different temperatures.
    -   Range of temperatures differed between species.
    -   There is an apparent relationship between pulse rate and
        temperature.
-   **Problem:** Are the species different, or are they just at
    different temperatures?
-   **Solution:** ANCOVA lets us adjust for the temperature effect to
    get a clearer, more powerful test of the species effect. .\
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false

# Create simulated cricket data
set.seed(456)
n <- 40
species <- rep(c("O. exclamationis", "O. niveus"), each = n/2)
temp <- c(rnorm(n/2, mean = 22, sd = 2), rnorm(n/2, mean = 24, sd = 2))
chirp_rate <- 40 + 2.5 * (temp - 23) + ifelse(species == "O. exclamationis", 10, 0) + rnorm(n, sd = 3)
cricket_data <- data.frame(species = species, temp = temp, chirp_rate = chirp_rate)

# Plot with regression lines by species
ggplot(cricket_data, aes(x = temp, y = chirp_rate, color = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Cricket Chirping Example",
       x = "Temperature (°C)",
       y = "Chirping Rate",
       color = "Species") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::
:::::

# ANCOVA Linear Model: Conceptual Framework

-   **The ANCOVA Model**
    -   Key concept: Dfference between "unadjusted" group means and
        "adjusted" means
-   **In this visualization:**
    -   **Group Means (asterisks):** The raw average X and Y for each
        group.
    -   Group A and Group B have different mean X values. Comparing
        directly misleading
    -   **Adjusted Means (triangles):** ANCOVA statistically "moves"
        each group mean along regression line to single, common X value
        (the overall mean X).
    -   ANCOVA compares heights (the Y values) of adjusted means.
    -   Adjustment "levels the playing field," estimating each group's
        mean if all groups had same average covariate value

# ANCOVA Model Visualization

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false
# Create diagram showing how ANCOVA works
set.seed(789)
x_range <- seq(2, 8, length.out = 100)
group_a <- data.frame(
  x = x_range,
  y = 2 + 0.7 * x_range,
  group = "Group A"
)
group_b <- data.frame(
  x = x_range,
  y = 5 + 0.7 * x_range,
  group = "Group B"
)
model_data <- rbind(group_a, group_b)

# Add points
set.seed(789)
points_a <- data.frame(
  x = runif(10, 2.5, 7.5),
  group = "Group A"
)
points_a$y <- 2 + 0.7 * points_a$x + rnorm(10, 0, 0.5)

points_b <- data.frame(
  x = runif(10, 2.5, 7.5),
  group = "Group B"
)
points_b$y <- 5 + 0.7 * points_b$x + rnorm(10, 0, 0.5)

points <- rbind(points_a, points_b)

# Calculate means for the groups
mean_x_a <- mean(points_a$x)
mean_y_a <- mean(points_a$y)
mean_x_b <- mean(points_b$x)
mean_y_b <- mean(points_b$y)
overall_mean_x <- mean(c(points_a$x, points_b$x))

# Calculate adjusted means
adjusted_y_a <- mean_y_a - 0.7 * (mean_x_a - overall_mean_x)
adjusted_y_b <- mean_y_b - 0.7 * (mean_x_b - overall_mean_x)

# Create the plot
ggplot() +
  # Add regression lines
  geom_line(data = model_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group), linewidth  = 1) +
  
  # Add points
  geom_point(data = points, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group), size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Add mean x and y for each group
  geom_point(aes(x = mean_x_a, y = mean_y_a), color = "darkred", size = 5, shape = 8) +
  geom_point(aes(x = mean_x_b, y = mean_y_b), color = "darkblue", size = 5, shape = 8) +
  
  # Add adjusted means at overall mean x triangle
  geom_point(aes(x = overall_mean_x, y = adjusted_y_a), color = "#F8766D", size = 5, shape = 18) +
  geom_point(aes(x = overall_mean_x, y = adjusted_y_b), color = "#00BFC4", size = 5, shape = 18) +
  
  # Add vertical lines to show adjustment
  geom_segment(aes(x = mean_x_a, y = mean_y_a, xend = overall_mean_x, yend = adjusted_y_a), 
               color = "#F8766D", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = mean_x_b, y = mean_y_b, xend = overall_mean_x, yend = adjusted_y_b), 
               color = "#00BFC4", linetype = "dashed") +
  
  # Add vertical line at overall mean x
  geom_vline(xintercept = overall_mean_x, linetype = "dotted") +
  
  # Labels
  annotate("text", x = mean_x_a - 0.2, y = mean_y_a + 1, label = "Group A\nMean", color = "darkred") +
  annotate("text", x = mean_x_b + 0.1, y = mean_y_b + 1, label = "Group B\nMean", color = "darkblue") +
  annotate("text", x = overall_mean_x + .4, y = adjusted_y_a - 0.5, 
           label = "Adjusted\nMean A", color = "#F8766D") +
  annotate("text", x = overall_mean_x + .2, y = adjusted_y_b - 0.8, 
           label = "Adjusted\nMean B", color = "#00BFC4") +
  
  # Theme
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "ANCOVA: Adjusting Means Based on Covariate",
       x = "Covariate (X)",
       y = "Response Variable (Y)",
       color = "Group") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::

# Mathematical Model for ANCOVA

-   Single-factor ANCOVA with
    -   factor A (p levels, i = 1 to p), a continuous covariate (x), and
        response variable (y):
        $Y_{ij} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta(X_{ij} - \bar{X}) + \varepsilon_{ij}$
-   Where:
    -   $Y_{ij}$ = response value for observation j in level i of factor
        A
    -   $\mu$ = overall mean
    -   $\alpha_i$ = effect of level i of factor A (what we test in
        ANOVA)
    -   $\beta$ = common regression slope relating Y to X (what we test
        in Regression)
    -   $X_{ij}$ = covariate value for observation j in level i of
        factor A
    -   $\bar{X}$ = mean value of covariate
    -   $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = error term

# ANCOVA Parameters Interpretation

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
-   **Interpretation of Parameters**
    -   $\mu$ = overall mean response
    -   $\alpha_i$ = effect of level i (difference between group's
        adjusted mean and overall mean)
    -   $\beta$ = pooled within-group regression coefficient (the slope)
    -   $X_{ij}$ = covariate value for observation j in group i
    -   $\bar{X}$ = overall mean of covariate
    -   $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = unexplained error
-   **Model assumes homogeneous slopes** (i.e., $\beta$ same for all
    groups) .
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false
# Create simple dataset to illustrate parameters
set.seed(321)
n <- 20
group <- rep(c("Group 1", "Group 2"), each = n/2)
x <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 1.2)
y <- 10 + ifelse(group == "Group 1", 2, -2) + 0.8 * (x - mean(x)) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.8)
param_data <- data.frame(group = group, x = x, y = y)

# Calculate overall mean
mu <- mean(y)
# Calculate group means
means_by_group <- aggregate(y ~ group, data = param_data, mean)
# Calculate effect of group 1
alpha_1 <- means_by_group$y[1] - mu
# Calculate effect of group 2
alpha_2 <- means_by_group$y[2] - mu

# Fit the model to get beta
model <- lm(y ~ group + x, data = param_data)
beta <- coef(model)["x"]

# Create plot
ggplot(param_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mu, linetype = "dashed", color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = max(x), y = mu + 0.2, label = "μ (overall mean)", hjust = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = min(x) + 0.4, y = mu + alpha_1, 
           label = paste("α1 =", round(alpha_1, 2)), color = "#F8766D") +
  annotate("text", x = min(x) + 0.4, y = mu + alpha_2, 
           label = paste("α2 =", round(alpha_2, 2)), color = "#00BFC4") +
  annotate("text", x = mean(x)+1, y = min(y), 
           label = paste("β =", round(beta, 2)), color = "black") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Covariate (X)", y = "Response (Y)") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::
:::::

# Building the ANCOVA Model in R

-   Remember `lm(y ~ predictor)`.
    -   ANCOVA combines the predictors from ANOVA and regression.
-   **Simple Linear Regression:**
    -   Predict y using a continuous variable x.
        -   `model_reg <- lm(y ~ x, data = mydata)`
-   **One-Way ANOVA:**
    -   We want to compare y among groups in a categorical factor A.
        -   `model_aov <- lm(y ~ A, data = mydata)`
-   **ANCOVA (Additive Model):**
    -   Compare y among groups in A, after accounting for effect of x.
    -   Add terms together
        -   `model_ancova <- lm(y ~ A + x, data = mydata)`
    -   Model assumes slope of y \~ x relationship is same for all
        groups in A.

# Building the ANCOVA Model in R

-   **ANCOVA (Interaction Model):**
    -   What if the slopes are different for each group?
    -   Test for interaction between factor and covariate.
        -   `model_int <- lm(y ~ A * x, data = mydata)`
    -   Shorthand for: `lm(y ~ A + x + A:x, data = mydata)`
    -   The `A:x` term tests if the slopes are different.

# ANCOVA in R: The 3-Step Workflow

-   **Step 1: Test for Homogeneity of Slopes - Always Start Here**

    -   Fit interaction model first - tests key assumption that all
        groups share same slope.

    ``` r
    # The asterisk * tests for both main effects AND the interaction
    model_int <- lm(y ~ A * x, data = mydata)
    ```

-   **Step 2: Examine the Interaction Term**

    -   ANOVA table for `model_int` and find the p-value for interaction
        term (`A:x`).

    ``` r
        Anova(model_int, type = "III")
    ```

    -   **If p \> 0.05** (e.g., p = 0.4) - interaction **IS NOT**
        significant - slopes are homogeneous (parallel).
    -   **If p \< 0.05** (e.g., p = 0.02): - interaction **IS**
        significant - slopes heterogeneous (NOT parallel).
    -   \*\* standard ANCOVA model (`y ~ A + x`) inappropriate\*\*
    -   Analysis stops here and interpret interaction (often most
        interesting result!)

# ANCOVA in R: The 3-Step Workflow

-   Step 3: Run the ANCOVA (Additive Model)

    -   If interaction not significant, run the simpler additive model
        the final ANCOVA model.

    ``` r
    # We use the + sign, which means "additive"
    model_ancova <- lm(y ~ A + x, data = mydata)
    # Get the final ANCOVA table
    Anova(model_ancova, type = "III")
    # Get the adjusted means
    library(emmeans)
    emmeans(model_ancova, "A")
    ```

# Analysis of Variance for ANCOVA: Partitioning

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 5
#| paged-print: false
# Create a visualization of partitioning variance in ANCOVA
set.seed(123)
n <- 60
group <- rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = n/3)
x <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 1.5)
# Create response with clear group differences and correlation with x
y <- 2 + 0.8 * x + ifelse(group == "A", 2, ifelse(group == "B", 1, 0)) + rnorm(n, sd = 1)
data <- data.frame(group = group, x = x, y = y)
# Fit models
model_null <- lm(y ~ 1, data = data)
model_cov <- lm(y ~ x, data = data)
model_full <- lm(y ~ x + group, data = data)
# Get predictions
data$pred_null <- predict(model_null)
data$pred_cov <- predict(model_cov)
data$pred_full <- predict(model_full)
# Get residuals
data$resid_null <- residuals(model_null)
data$resid_cov <- residuals(model_cov)
data$resid_full <- residuals(model_full)
# Calculate SS
ss_total <- sum((data$y - mean(data$y))^2)
ss_cov <- sum((data$pred_cov - mean(data$y))^2)
ss_group_adj <- sum((data$pred_full - data$pred_cov)^2)
ss_residual <- sum(data$resid_full^2)
# Create labels
ss_labels <- c(
  paste("SS_Total =", round(ss_total, 1)),
  paste("SS_Covariate =", round(ss_cov, 1)),
  paste("SS_Group(adj) =", round(ss_group_adj, 1)),
  paste("SS_Residual =", round(ss_residual, 1))
)
# Create a stacked bar chart of SS components
ss_data <- data.frame(
  component = c("SS_Covariate", "SS_Group(adj)", "SS_Residual"),
  value = c(ss_cov, ss_group_adj, ss_residual),
  percentage = c(ss_cov/ss_total, ss_group_adj/ss_total, ss_residual/ss_total) * 100
)
# Plot 1: Partitioning of SS
partitioning_plot1 <- ggplot(ss_data, aes(x = "", y = value, fill = component)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(percentage, 1), "%")), 
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Partitioning of Variance in ANCOVA",
       x = "",
       y = "Sum of Squares",
       fill = "Component") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# Plot 2: Visualization of model fit
plot_data <- data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(y, pred_null, pred_cov, pred_full),
               names_to = "type", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(type = factor(type, levels = c("y", "pred_null", "pred_cov", "pred_full"),
                      labels = c("Data", "Null Model", "Covariate Model", "Full Model")))
# Sample 20 points for clarity
set.seed(42)
sample_indices <- sample(1:n, 20)
plot_data_sample <- plot_data %>% 
  filter(row_number() %in% rep(sample_indices, 4))
partitioning_plot2 <- ggplot(plot_data_sample, aes(x = x, y = value, color = type)) +
  geom_point(data = filter(plot_data_sample, type == "Data"), size = 3) +
  geom_line(data = filter(plot_data_sample, type != "Data"), aes(group = interaction(type, group)), linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~group) +
  labs(title = "Model Fits in ANCOVA",
       x = "Covariate (X)",
       y = "Response (Y)",
       color = "Model") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
# Combine plots
partitioning_plot1 + partitioning_plot2 + plot_layout(ncol = 1, heights = c(1, 2))
```
:::

# ANOVA Table for ANCOVA

The ANOVA table for a single-factor ANCOVA has these components:

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
# Create a tibble with ANOVA table information
anova_table <- tibble(
  Source = c("Factor A (adjusted)", "Covariate", "Residual", "Total"),
  df = c("(p-1)", "1", "n-p-1", "n-1"),
  `Sum of Squares` = c("SS_A(adj)", "SS_Covariate", "SS_Residual", "SS_Total"),
  `Mean Square` = c("MS_A(adj) = SS_A(adj)/(p-1)", "MS_Covariate = SS_Covariate/1", 
                   "MS_Residual = SS_Residual/(n-p-1)", ""),
  `F-ratio` = c("MS_A(adj)/MS_Residual", "MS_Covariate/MS_Residual", "", ""),
  `Expected MS` = c("σ² + n∑α²/(p-1)", "σ² + β²∑(X-X̄)²", "σ²", "")
)

# Create
anova_table

```
:::

# Null Hypotheses in ANCOVA

### Homogeneity of Slopes (Test this first!)

$$H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = ... = \beta_p$$

Are the regression slopes the same for all groups?

Test with the `A:x` interaction term in `lm(y ~ A * x)`.

### Treatment Effect (adjusted for covariate)

$$H_0: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = ... = \alpha_p = 0$$

Are the adjusted group means equal?

Test with F = MS_A(adj)/MS_Residual (the 'A' term in `lm(y ~ A + x)`).

### Covariate Effect

$$H_0: \beta = 0$$

Is there a relationship between the covariate and the response?

Test with F = MS_Covariate/MS_Residual (the 'x' term in
`lm(y ~ A + x)`).

# Visualization of Homogeneity of Slopes

**Parallel vs. Non-Parallel Slopes**

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false
# Create two datasets - one with parallel slopes, one without
set.seed(123)
n <- 40

# 1. Parallel slopes (homogeneous)
x1 <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 1.5)
group1 <- rep(c("A", "B"), each = n/2)
y1 <- 2 + 0.7 * x1 + ifelse(group1 == "A", 2, 0) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.8)
data1 <- data.frame(x = x1, y = y1, group = group1, type = "Homogeneous Slopes (p > 0.05)")

# 2. Non-parallel slopes (heterogeneous)
x2 <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 1.5)
group2 <- rep(c("A", "B"), each = n/2)
y2 <- 2 + ifelse(group2 == "A", 1.2 * x2, 0.4 * x2) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.8)
data2 <- data.frame(x = x2, y = y2, group = group2, type = "Heterogeneous Slopes (p < 0.05)")

# Combine datasets
all_data <- rbind(data1, data2)

# Fit models for each dataset
model1_int <- lm(y ~ x * group, data = data1) # Homogeneous
model2_int <- lm(y ~ x * group, data = data2) # Heterogeneous

# Extract p-values for interactions
# [3, "Pr(>F)"] targets the interaction term 'x:group'
p1 <- anova(model1_int)[3, "Pr(>F)"] # p = 0.274
p2 <- anova(model2_int)[3, "Pr(>F)"] # p = 0.002

# --- START OF FIX ---

# 1. Create a dedicated data frame for annotations
# This links each label to the correct 'type' for faceting
annotations <- data.frame(
  type = c("Homogeneous Slopes (p > 0.05)", "Heterogeneous Slopes (p < 0.05)"),
  label = c(paste("Interaction p =", round(p1, 3)), # p = 0.274
            paste("Interaction p =", round(p2, 3))), # p = 0.002
  x_pos = 3,    # X-coordinate for text
  y_pos = 11.5  # Y-coordinate for text
)

# 2. Plot with geom_text() and corrected subtitle
ggplot(all_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~type) +
  labs(
    title = "Testing Homogeneity of Slopes (The Interaction)",
    # CORRECTED SUBTITLE: "Heterogeneous" (p=0.002) is on the left
    subtitle = "Left: Interaction IS significant (p < 0.05). | Right: Interaction is NOT significant (p > 0.05). Use ANCOVA.",
    x = "Covariate (X)",
    y = "Response (Y)",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  # Use geom_text() instead of annotate()
  # It will use the 'type' column from 'annotations' to plot in the correct panel
  geom_text(
    data = annotations,
    aes(x = x_pos, y = y_pos, label = label),
    hjust = 0, # Left-align text
    inherit.aes = FALSE # CRITICAL: stops inheriting 'color = group'
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold")
  )
```
:::

# Partridge Example: Data Overview

**ANCOVA on Longevity of Male Fruitflies**

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| paged-print: false
# We've already loaded this, but good practice
partridge <- read.csv("data/partridge.csv")

# Create better names for treatments
partridge$treatment <- factor(partridge$TREATMEN,
                            levels = 1:5,
                            labels = c("No females", 
                                       "One virgin female daily",
                                       "Eight virgin females daily",
                                       "One inseminated female daily",
                                       "Eight inseminated females daily"))

# 2. Create a plot of the data showing relationship
ggplot(partridge, aes(x = THORAX, y = LONGEV, color = treatment)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) + # se=FALSE to see lines clearly
  labs(title = "Relationship between Thorax Length and Longevity",
       x = "Thorax Length (mm)",
       y = "Longevity (days)",
       color = "Treatment") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::

# Partridge Example: Step 1 - Test Homogeneity

-   **Testing Homogeneity of Slopes**
    -   First, fit interaction model.
    -   The p-value for the interaction term (THORAX:treatment) is xl.
    -   Since this value is \> 0.05, we conclude the slopes are
        homogeneous.
    -   We can proceed to Step 3 and fit the simpler additive model.

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
# Step 1: Fit model with interaction term
homo_slopes_model <- lm(LONGEV ~ THORAX * treatment, data = partridge)
# Step 2: Test significance of interaction using Type III SS
Anova(homo_slopes_model, type = "III")
# Extract the p-value for interaction
p_interaction <- Anova(homo_slopes_model, type = "III")["THORAX:treatment", "Pr(>F)"]
```
:::

# Partridge Example: Step 3 ANCOVA Analysis

-   Since the interaction was not significant, we re-run the model
    without it.
    -   **Interpretation:**
        -   **THORAX:** covariate highly significant
        -   thorax length explains significant amount of variance in
            longevity
        -   **treatment:** treatment effect, after adjusting for thorax
            length, is highly significant

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| paged-print: false
# Step 3: Fit the ANCOVA model (additive model)
ancova_model <- lm(LONGEV ~ THORAX + treatment, data = partridge)
# View the final ANCOVA table
Anova(ancova_model, type = "III")
```
:::

# Partridge Example: Getting Adjusted Means

-   **The ANOVA table tells us the treatments are different, but not
    which ones.**
    -   Use `emmeans` package to get the adjusted means / perform
        pairwise comparisons

    -   The "estimated marginal mean" (or adjusted mean)

    -   predicted longevity for each group, assuming an average thorax
        length

    -   This is the "level playing field" comparison.

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
# Get adjusted means using emmeans
library(emmeans)
adjusted_means <- emmeans(ancova_model, "treatment")
adjusted_means
```
:::

# Partridge Example: Pairwise Comparisons

-   **Pairwise Comparisons of Adjusted Means to see which groups
    differ**
    -   **Interpretation:**

        -   The `pairs()` output gives p-values for all combinations.

        -   The plot shows these comparisons visually. Red arrows
            connect any groups that are NOT significantly different.

        -   Here, "One virgin female" and "One inseminated female" are
            not different from each other, but all other comparisons are
            significant.

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false
# Pairwise comparisons of adjusted means
pairs(adjusted_means, adjust = "tukey")
```
:::

# Partridge Example: Pairwise Comparisons

-   **Pairwise Comparisons of Adjusted Means to see which groups
    differ**
    -   **Interpretation:**

        -   The `pairs()` output gives p-values for all combinations.

        -   The plot shows these comparisons visually. Red arrows
            connect any groups that are NOT significantly different.

        -   Here, "One virgin female" and "One inseminated female" are
            not different from each other, but all other comparisons are
            significant.

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
# Plot adjusted means with confidence intervals
plot(adjusted_means, comparisons = TRUE) + 
  labs(title = "Adjusted Means by Treatment",
       x = "Adjusted Longevity (days)",
       y = "Treatment") +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::

# Visualizing ANCOVA Results

**Visualization Options for ANCOVA**

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 7
#| paged-print: false
# Create a 2x2 panel of different visualization options
mean_thorax <- mean(partridge$THORAX)
adj_means_data <- as.data.frame(adjusted_means)

# 1. Raw data with regression lines
ancova_vis_plot1 <- ggplot(partridge, aes(x = THORAX, y = LONGEV, color = treatment)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "1. Raw Data w Regressions",
       x = "Thorax Length (mm)",
       y = "Longevity (days)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# 2. Add adjusted means reference line
ancova_vis_plot2 <- ggplot(partridge, aes(x = THORAX, y = LONGEV, color = treatment)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean_thorax, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = mean_thorax + 0.02, y = min(partridge$LONGEV) + 5, 
           label = "Mean X", angle = 90, hjust = 0) +
  labs(title = "2. Reference Line @ Mean Cov.",
       x = "Thorax Length (mm)",
       y = "Longevity (days)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# 3. Adjusted means as points on the vertical line
ancova_vis_plot3 <- ggplot(partridge, aes(x = THORAX, y = LONGEV, color = treatment)) + 
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean_thorax, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(data = adj_means_data, aes(x = mean_thorax, y = emmean, color = treatment), 
             size = 4, shape = 18) +
  labs(title = "3. Adjusted Means @ Mean Covar.",
       x = "Thorax Length (mm)",
       y = "Longevity (days)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# 4. Bar chart of adjusted means with error bars
ancova_vis_plot4 <- ggplot(adj_means_data, aes(x = treatment, y = emmean, fill = treatment)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL), width = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "4. Adjusted Means",
       x = "Treatment",
       y = "Adjusted Longevity (days)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1))

# Combine plots
(ancova_vis_plot1 + ancova_vis_plot2) / (ancova_vis_plot3 + ancova_vis_plot4) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") +
  plot_annotation(title = "Visualization Options for ANCOVA Results",
                 theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, hjust = 0.5)))
```
:::

# What if Slopes are HETEROGENEOUS? (p \< 0.05)

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
-   **Example: Sea Urchin Shrinking w/ Heterogeneous Slopes**
-   Constable (1993) studied sea urchin test shrinking:
    -   Compared suture widths between treatments

    -   Three groups: high food, low food, initial sample

    -   Covariate: body volume (cube root transformed)
-   The analysis showed:
    -   Significant interaction between treatment and covariate

    -   Heterogeneous slopes across treatments
-   Relationship between body volume and suture width depends on
    treatment group
-   **Standard ANCOVA is invalid.**\
    .\
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false
# Create simulated sea urchin data based on description
set.seed(345)
n <- 72  # 24 urchins per group

# Create data frame
treatments <- rep(c("Initial", "Low Food", "High Food"), each = n/3)
volume <- c(
  runif(n/3, 10, 40),  # Initial
  runif(n/3, 10, 40),  # Low Food
  runif(n/3, 10, 40)   # High Food
)

# Create suture width with different slopes for each treatment
suture_width <- ifelse(
  treatments == "Initial", 0.05 + 0.002 * volume,
  ifelse(
    treatments == "Low Food", 0.04 + 0.0005 * volume,
    0.02 + 0.003 * volume  # High Food
  )
) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.01)

urchin_data <- data.frame(treatment = treatments, volume = volume, suture_width = suture_width)

# Plot the data with regression lines
ggplot(urchin_data, aes(x = volume, y = suture_width, color = treatment)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Sea Urchin Suture Width vs. Volume",
       subtitle = "Example with Heterogeneous Slopes",
       x = "Cube Root Body Volume",
       y = "Suture Width (mm)",
       color = "Treatment") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::
:::::

# Handling Heterogeneous Slopes

If the interaction term is significant (p \< 0.05):

1.  **Report interaction!** often more interesting biological result
    than simple mean differences

2.  **Analyze slopes:** Use emmeans to get slopes for each group.

    ``` r
    emtrends(model_int, "treatment", var = "x")
    ```

3.  **Johnson-Neyman procedure:** advanced method to identify regions of
    covariate (X-axis) where groups are (and are not) significantly
    different.

4.  **Separate regressions:** Analyze each group separately (less
    powerful).

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r urchin-slopes-test}
    #| echo: false
    # Fit model with interaction
    urchin_model <- lm(suture_width ~ volume * treatment, data = urchin_data)
    Anova(urchin_model, type = 3)


```
:::

# Interpretation of Heterogeneous Slopes

-   With heterogeneous slopes, interpretation shifts from "adjusted
    means" to "regions of significance":
    -   **Initial \> Low Food** when cube root body volume \> 2.95\
        For large urchins, initial sample has wider sutures than low
        food urchins
    -   **High Food \> Initial** when cube root body volume \> 1.81\
        For most urchins, high food treatment results in wider sutures
        than initial samples.
    -   **High Food \> Low Food** when cube root body volume \> 2.07\
        For most medium to large urchins, high food results in wider
        sutures than low food.
-   Biological interpretation is food regime affects suture width
    differently depending on urchin size

# Assumptions of ANCOVA

-   **Key Assumptions**

    1.  **Independence of observations**\
        Samples are random and independent.
    2.  **Normal distribution of residuals**\
        Check with QQ plots.
    3.  **Homogeneity of variances (Homoscedasticity)**\
        Equal variances across groups.\
        Check with residual vs. fitted plots.
    4.  **Linearity**\
        A linear relationship exists between Y and X within each group.\
        Check with scatterplots (we did this!).
    5.  **Homogeneity of regression slopes (The critical one!)**\
        Regression slopes are equal across all groups.\
        We tested this first! (the A:x interaction).

# Checking Assumptions in R

-   For assumptions 2 and 3, we check the final model (the additive one,
    if appropriate).
-   **Interpretation:**
    -   **Residuals vs. Fitted:** The red line should be roughly flat
        at 0. Looks good.
    -   **Normal Q-Q:** Points should fall along the dashed line. Looks
        good.
    -   **Scale-Location:** Red line should be flat (tests homogeneity
        of variance). Looks good.
    -   **Residuals vs. Leverage:** No points should be in the
        top/bottom right (high leverage/influence). Looks good.
-   **Conclusion:** The assumptions are met.

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 5
# Fit ANCOVA model for partridge data
ancova_model <- lm(LONGEV ~ THORAX + treatment, data = partridge)

# Create a 2x2 panel of diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(ancova_model)
```
:::

# Robust ANCOVA Approaches

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
-   **Non-Parametric Alternatives**
-   When ANCOVA assumptions are badly violated (especially normality or
    homogeneity of variance), consider:
-   **Rank Transformation (Simplest)**
    -   Rank transform both Y and X variables.
    -   Run standard ANCOVA on the ranked data.
    -   `lm(rank(y) ~ rank(x) + A)`
-   **Bootstrapping**
    -   Use bootstrapping to estimate parameters without assuming
        normality.
-   **Quantile Regression**
    -   Models relationships at different quantiles (e.g., the median)
        instead of the mean.
-   **Permutation Tests**
    -   Randomization tests of treatment effects. .
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
# Example of rank-transformed ANCOVA
partridge$rank_LONGEV <- rank(partridge$LONGEV)
partridge$rank_THORAX <- rank(partridge$THORAX)

# Fit ANCOVA on ranked data
rank_ancova <- lm(rank_LONGEV ~ 
                  rank_THORAX + treatment, 
                data = partridge)

Anova(rank_ancova, type = "III")

# Compare p-values for treatment effect
cat("P-value (parametric):", 
   Anova(ancova_model, type="III")["treatment", "Pr(>F)"],  "\n")
cat("P-value (rank-based):", 
    Anova(rank_ancova, type="III")["treatment", "Pr(>F)"])
```

**Note:** The results are very similar, which gives us more confidence
in our original parametric test.
:::
:::::

# Writing Up ANCOVA Results

**Scientific Writing Example**

Here's how you might write up ANCOVA results for publication:

> "We analyzed the effects of mating strategy on male fruitfly longevity
> using analysis of covariance (ANCOVA), with thorax length as a
> covariate. Before conducting the main analysis, we tested the
> homogeneity of slopes assumption and found no significant interaction
> between treatment and thorax length (F₄,₁₁₅ = xxx , indicating that
> the effect of body size on longevity was consistent across treatments.
>
> The ANCOVA revealed significant effects of both treatment (F₄,₁₁₉ =
> xxxx and thorax length (F₁,₁₁₉ = xxxx, P \< 0.001) on longevity.
> Thorax length was positively associated with longevity...
>
> After adjusting for body size, males with no female partners lived
> significantly longer (adjusted mean ± SE: xxxx\` days) than males in
> any other treatment group. Pairwise comparisons using Tukey's HSD test
> indicated significant differences between most treatment groups (P \<
> 0.05) except between the 'One virgin female' and 'One inseminated
> female' groups (P = x)."

## Publication Quality Figure

::: {.panel column="screen"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| paged-print: false
# Create an example of a publication-quality figure for the partridge data
adj_means_data <- as.data.frame(adjusted_means)
mean_thorax <- mean(partridge$THORAX)

# Get prediction data for each treatment across range of thorax lengths
thorax_range <- seq(min(partridge$THORAX), max(partridge$THORAX), length.out = 100)
pred_data <- expand.grid(thorax = thorax_range, treatment = levels(partridge$treatment))
pred_data$LONGEV <- predict(ancova_model, newdata = data.frame(THORAX = pred_data$thorax, 
                                                         treatment = pred_data$treatment))

# Plot
pub_plot1 <- ggplot() +
  # Raw data with transparency
  geom_point(data = partridge, aes(x = THORAX, y = LONGEV, color = treatment), 
             alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  # Regression lines
  geom_line(data = pred_data, aes(x = thorax, y = LONGEV, color = treatment), 
            size = 1) +
  # Reference line at mean thorax
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(partridge$THORAX), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  # Adjusted means at mean thorax length
  geom_point(data = adj_means_data, 
             aes(x = mean(partridge$THORAX), y = emmean, color = treatment), 
             size = 4, shape = 18) +
  # Error bars for adjusted means
  geom_errorbar(data = adj_means_data,
                aes(x = mean(partridge$THORAX), y = emmean, 
                    ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL, color = treatment),
                width = 0.02) +
  # Labels
  labs(title = "Mating effect-Male Fruitfly Longevity",
       x = "Thorax Length (mm)",
       y = "Longevity (days)",
       color = "Treatment") +
  # Theme
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "gray80"),
        plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"))

# Adjusted means bar chart
pub_plot2 <- ggplot(adj_means_data, aes(x = treatment, y = emmean, fill = treatment)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL), width = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Adjusted Mean Longevity by Treatment",
       subtitle = "Means adjusted for thorax length",
       x = "Treatment",
       y = "Adjusted Longevity (days)",
       fill = "Treatment") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "gray80"),
        plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1))

# Combine plots
pub_plot1 + pub_plot2 + plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1))
```
:::

# Summary

-   **Purpose:**
    -   ANCOVA combines regression and ANOVA.
    -   It increases power by accounting for a continuous covariate.
    -   It adjusts for confounds by comparing "adjusted means."
-   **The Analysis Workflow**
    1.  **Step 1:** Always test for homogeneity of slopes first
        (`lm(y ~ A * x)`).
    2.  **Step 2:** If slopes are homogeneous (p \> 0.05), proceed to
        the additive model (`lm(y ~ A + x)`).
    3.  **Step 3:** If slopes are heterogeneous (p \< 0.05), STOP and
        interpret the interaction.
-   **Interpretation**
    -   Use `car::Anova(model, type = "III")` to get the ANOVA table.
    -   Use `emmeans()` to get adjusted means and pairwise comparisons.
-   **Assumptions**
    -   Independence of observations

    -   Normal distribution of residuals

    -   Homogeneity of variances

    -   Linearity of relationships within groups

    -   Homogeneity of regression slopes (The most important one!)
